title: Introduction to OOP
summary: Model real-world things as objects with attributes and behaviors using classes

sections.title: Classes are blueprints, objects are the actual things
sections.content: Think of a class as an architectural blueprint for a house. The blueprint defines that every house has a certain number of bedrooms, a kitchen, and doors. But the blueprint isn't a house you can live in — you must build actual houses from it. Each built house is an object (also called an instance). One blueprint can create many different houses. Similarly, a Python class defines what attributes (data, like color or size) and methods (behaviors, like open_door or turn_on_light) each object of that class will have.

sections.title: A simple Car class
sections.content: This class models a car with a brand, model, and a method to start the engine
sections.code:
# Define a class called Car — the blueprint for all car objects
class Car:
    # __init__ is the constructor — runs automatically when creating a new car
    # self refers to the specific car being created (like "this car")
    def __init__(self, brand, model, year):
        # Store the brand argument as an attribute of this car
        self.brand = brand  # Each car gets its own brand
        self.model = model  # Each car gets its own model
        self.year = year  # Each car gets its own year
        self.is_running = False  # Default: engine is off

    # A method is a function inside a class — defines what a car can do
    def start_engine(self):
        # Set the is_running attribute from False to True
        self.is_running = True
        return f"{self.brand} {self.model} engine started!"

# Create actual car objects from the blueprint
my_car = Car("Toyota", "Corolla", 2022)  # Calls __init__ automatically
print(my_car.start_engine())  # Call the method on my_car
print("Brand:", my_car.brand)  # Access an attribute directly
# Expected output:
# Toyota Corolla engine started!
# Brand: Toyota

sections.title: Self, init, and methods — the OOP trinity
sections.content: The __init__ method (short for initialize) is the constructor. Python calls it automatically when you create an object. The self parameter is the trickiest concept for beginners: it's the object itself, passed automatically. When you write my_car.start_engine(), Python secretly does Car.start_engine(my_car) — self becomes my_car. This is why every method needs self as its first parameter. Attributes (self.brand, self.model) are like sticky notes attached to the object that store its data for as long as the object exists.

sections.title: Forgetting self is the #1 OOP mistake
sections.content: Beginners often leave out self in method definitions or attribute assignments. Wrong: def start_engine(): (no self) causes TypeError. Wrong: brand = brand (no self.) creates a local variable, not an attribute. Correct: def start_engine(self): and self.brand = brand. Each method in a class must have self as the first parameter.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A pet store needs a class to track animals. Create a Pet class with attributes: name, species, and age. Include a method called describe() that returns a string like 'Buddy is a 3-year-old dog.' Then create two pet objects and call describe() on each.
hints: What method runs automatically when you create a new Pet object?
How do you make sure describe() can access the pet's own name and species?
